title: Life Sciences Virtual Investor Forum Presentations Now Available for Online Viewing
content:
NEW YORK, March  13, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Virtual Investor Conferences, the leading proprietary investor conference series, today announced that presentations from the Life Sciences Virtual Investor Forum, held March 11th – 12th, are now available for on-demand viewing.

The forum featured CEO chats and company presentations from leadership teams representing innovative companies across the biotechnology, medical device, and pharmaceutical sectors, offering investors insights into emerging technologies, strategic initiatives, and growth opportunities within the life sciences industry.

REGISTER AND VIEW PRESENTATIONS HERE

The presentations will be available 24/7 for 90 days. Investors, advisors, and analysts may download investor materials from the company’s resource section.

Select companies are accepting 1x1 management meeting requests through March 17th.

Please Schedule 1x1 Meetings here

March 11th & 12th PresentationTicker(s)SeaStar Medical Holding Corporation(NASDAQ: ICU)Nasus Pharma Ltd.(NYSE: NSRX)Tonix Pharmaceuticals Holding Corp.(NASDAQ: TNXP)Jupiter Neurosciences, Inc.(NASDAQ: JUNS)Bora Pharmaceuticals Co., Ltd.(OTCQX: BORAY | TWSE: 6472)MetaVia Inc.(NASDAQ: MTVA)Phio Pharmaceuticals Corp.(NASDAQ: PHIO)Avicanna Inc.(OTCQX: AVCNF | TSX: AVCN)EMVision Medical Devices Ltd.(OTC: EMVDF | ASX: EMV)Aethlon Medical, Inc.(NASDAQ: AEMD)Dyadic International, Inc.(NASDAQ: DYAI)Jaguar Health, Inc.(NASDAQ: JAGX)Beyond Air, Inc.(NASDAQ: XAIR) Cadrenal Therapeutics, Inc.(NASDAQ: CVKD)Amplia Therapeutics Ltd.(OTCQB: INNMF | ASX: ATX)

To facilitate investor relations scheduling and to view a complete calendar of Virtual Investor Conferences, please visit www.virtualinvestorconferences.com.

About Virtual Investor Conferences®

Virtual Investor Conferences (VIC) is the leading proprietary investor conference series that provides an interactive forum for publicly traded companies to seamlessly present directly to investors.

Providing a real-time investor engagement solution, VIC is specifically designed to offer companies more efficient investor access. Replicating the components of an on-site investor conference, VIC offers companies enhanced capabilities to connect with investors, schedule targeted one-on-one meetings and enhance their presentations with dynamic video content. Accelerating the next level of investor engagement, Virtual Investor Conferences delivers leading investor communications to a global network of retail and institutional investors.

SVP Corporate Services, Investor Access

title: Life Sciences Virtual Investor Forum Agenda Announced for March 11th & 12th
content:
Virtual Investor Conferences

Company Executives Share Strategic Vision and Engage with Investors Live at VirtualInvestorConferences.com

NEW YORK, March 10, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Virtual Investor Conferences, the leading proprietary investor conference series, today announced the agenda for the Life Sciences Virtual Investor Forum, taking place March 11–12, 2026.

Individual investors, institutional investors, advisors, and analysts are invited to attend.

REGISTER HERE

Investors are encouraged to pre-register and run the online system check to expedite participation and receive event updates. Participation is free, and attendees may watch live company presentations and schedule one-on-one meetings with management teams.

Schedule 1x1 meetings here

“We are delighted to highlight today’s innovators from the Life Science sector,” said Jason Paltrowitz, EVP of Corporate Services at OTC Markets Group. “Our Virtual Investor Conferences continue to provide an efficient platform and unique opportunity for these companies to engage to a broader investor base and communicate their strategies.”

title: Aethlon Medical signals faster clinical trial enrollment amid Hemopurifier advancements
content:
Earnings Call Insights: Aethlon Medical (AEMD) Q3 2026

MANAGEMENT VIEW

 	* James Frakes, CEO and CFO, stated that Aethlon Medical continues to execute its strategy of advancing the Hemopurifier platform while maintaining disciplined cost control, emphasizing "continued enrollment and treatment progress in our Australian oncology trial; ongoing expansion of our extracellular vesicle, or EV, research platform, supporting the Hemopurifier as a potential multi-indication therapeutic; advancement of work evaluating Hemopurifier compatibility with a simplified blood treatment system that could expand future clinical and commercial flexibility; and sustained operating expense reductions on a year-to-date basis compared to the prior year." 
 	* Dr. Steven Larosa, Chief Medical Officer, provided an update on the Australian oncology trial, noting the completion of two Hemopurifier treatments in two participants in the second cohort, with a third patient enrolled and scheduled for treatment by the end of February. Dr. Larosa explained that "once the 3 patients have completed treatment in Cohort 2, safety data will be presented to an independent Data Safety Monitoring Board. We are targeting late March for this meeting."
 	* Frakes reported, "As of December 31, 2025, we had a cash balance of approximately $7 million. Our consolidated operating expenses for the 3 months ended December 31, 2025 were approximately $2.06 million, up $250,000 or 13.6% compared to the same period last year."

OUTLOOK

 	* Management is targeting a Data Safety Monitoring Board review in late March to determine whether to advance to Cohort 3, where patients would receive three Hemopurifier treatments in a week. The company highlighted an increase in potential trial participants due to new recruitment strategies.
 	* The company plans further research into the Hemopurifier’s compatibility with simplified blood treatment systems, which could facilitate broader use in oncology and infusion centers.

FINANCIAL RESULTS

 	* Frakes stated that operating loss for the quarter increased to $2.06 million, compared to $1.81 million in the prior year period. Other income, primarily interest income on cash balances, was $44,000, slightly lower than the $60,000 recorded in the same quarter last year.
 	* For the nine-month period, operating expenses decreased to $5.36 million, down $1.98 million or 27% from $7.34 million last year. This reflects lower payroll, general and administrative costs, and professional fees.

Q&A

 	* Marla Marin, Zacks Investment Research: Asked for confirmation on the trial schedule and the impact of moving from one to two Hemopurifier treatments. Dr. Larosa confirmed, "It's the same follow-up period... instead of getting 1 Hemopurifier treatment in Cohort 1 and Cohort 2, they get 2."
 	* Marin also inquired about the potential impact of the SLAMB system. Dr. Larosa described the benefits of moving to a "much smaller single-lumen catheter... a big change in terms of the invasiveness of the catheter" and expanded accessibility in oncology units.
 	* Marin questioned cost-effectiveness in R&D. Frakes responded, "We're trying to keep costs down as much as we can, yet still advance. So we're trying to obtain samples just for shipping costs basically, to the extent we can. We're trying to do the work in-house with our small scientific staff."
 	* Jeremy Pearlman, Maxim Group: Asked about the timeline for advancing to Cohort 3 and the readiness of new participants. Dr. Larosa noted an "exciting" uptick in potential participants and the ability to quickly enroll new patients once the protocol allows.
 	* Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: Asked whether Cohort 2 results could obviate the need for Cohort 3. Dr. Larosa explained, "I think we would be really hamstringing ourselves to stop at 2 and not investigate 3 because I have every reason to believe 3 could potentially be superior to 2 in terms of EV removal or T cells."

SENTIMENT ANALYSIS

 	* Analysts asked detailed questions about trial logistics, recruitment, and device integration, indicating a cautiously optimistic but probing tone about the company’s execution and future plans.
 	* Management maintained a confident and transparent tone, especially regarding clinical progress and cost discipline, but used caution in discussing forward steps, often referencing the need for more data or regulatory input.
 	* Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment showed increased optimism, attributable to better recruitment progress and operational execution.

QUARTER-OVER-QUARTER COMPARISON

 	* Guidance language remains focused on disciplined cost management and advancing the Hemopurifier trial, with a new emphasis this quarter on accelerated patient recruitment through digital outreach and prescreening partnerships.
 	* Analysts shifted from concerns about slow enrollment and operational challenges to questions about the pace of trial advancement and broader clinical integration.
 	* Operating expenses rose this quarter due to higher payroll, but year-to-date cost controls remain a focus.
 	* Management's confidence appears stronger, with both Frakes and Larosa frequently referencing increased momentum and improved trial logistics compared to the prior quarter.

RISKS AND CONCERNS

 	* Management noted the ongoing need for favorable safety outcomes and regulatory approval before progressing to further trial cohorts.
 	* Integration of the Hemopurifier with new devices (such as the SLAMB system) still requires compatibility validation and regulatory review, which could delay broader clinical adoption.
 	* The company remains focused on Australia for clinical progress, explicitly ruling out a return to trials in India at this stage, citing the need to avoid distraction and additional expense.

FINAL TAKEAWAY

Aethlon Medical reported solid progress in its Australian oncology trial and clinical research pipeline, supported by disciplined cost management and enhanced recruitment efforts. Management underscored the advancing pace of trial enrollment and highlighted research initiatives that could expand the Hemopurifier’s future clinical applications. Investors can expect upcoming safety data review in late March, which will determine the next steps for the company’s lead clinical program.
